skill.name: deeptools
skill.description: Guides deepTools analysis for sequencing data, including BAM to bigWig conversion, quality control, normalization, and heatmap workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and generate deepTools workflows for NGS quality control, coverage tracks, comparisons, and visualizations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution, credential, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown command examples or genomics terms such as SAM and RPKM. The confirmed risk is that scripts/workflow_generator.py writes runnable shell workflows with unescaped user-supplied paths and parameters, so publication should include a warning about trusted inputs.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped Parameters in Generated Shell Workflows
medium_findings.description: The workflow generator embeds user-supplied BAM, BED, output directory, and output file values directly into bash script templates. Malicious shell metacharacters in those values could execute when the generated script is run, although the generator itself does not execute the workflow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The vulnerable interpolation is visible in generated bash assignments and commands, and the same file accepts those values from command-line arguments. Risk depends on a user running the generated script, so this is a confirmed but contextual issue.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Code Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are documentation examples for deepTools, samtools, grep, awk, and related genomics tooling. They are not hidden execution paths, but users are instructed to run commands as part of legitimate analysis workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown code blocks or command references, not executable code inside the skill runtime. The skill purpose is command-line bioinformatics guidance, so command examples are expected.

low_findings.title: Credential and Network Scanner Hits Are Genomics Term Collisions
low_findings.description: Critical Windows SAM database findings are false positives on SAM alignment flags, and network scanning findings appear to be collisions with bioinformatics command names or parameters. No credential access or network reconnaissance intent was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines refer to SAM-format read filtering in genomic alignment files. I found no evidence of Windows SAM access, password extraction, or network scanning behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Documentation Word Matches
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography detections match unrelated words and scientific terms, including description text, RPKM normalization, and sortRegions descend. No cryptographic operations or unsafe hashing code was found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content discusses genome-size calculation, normalization methods, display modes, and argparse formatting. These are semantic false positives rather than cryptographic APIs.

low_findings.title: Expected Local File Writes in Workflow Generator
low_findings.description: The workflow generator writes generated bash scripts to a user-selected output path. This is expected functionality, but users should avoid running it with untrusted paths because existing files could be overwritten.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and intentional. The risk is limited because the local user chooses the output path, but there is no overwrite protection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Only
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to official deepTools documentation links in reference material. They do not trigger network requests or send local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain Markdown references. No code path fetches these links or transmits user data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Bash Script Uses Unsanitized Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated workflow templates assign user parameters into shell variables and later expand them without robust quoting. Shell-special characters in filenames or directories can change generated script behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in bash templates and variable expansion. The generator is useful, but it should quote or escape inputs before creating runnable scripts.

content.value_statement: Sequencing workflows often require many deepTools commands and careful normalization choices. This skill helps plan, validate, and generate reproducible NGS analysis steps.
actual_capabilities: Recommends deepTools commands for BAM to bigWig and bedGraph coverage conversion.
Guides ChIP-seq, RNA-seq, and ATAC-seq workflow selection from included references.
Generates template shell workflows for common deepTools analyses.
Validates BAM, bigWig, and BED inputs with included helper scripts.
Explains normalization choices such as RPGC, CPM, RPKM, BPM, and readCount.
Provides command patterns for correlation, PCA, fingerprint, heatmap, and profile plots.
limitations: It does not install deepTools or required genome analysis dependencies automatically.
Generated shell scripts should be reviewed before execution, especially when paths come from untrusted sources.
It cannot confirm biological validity without metadata, controls, genome assembly, and experiment design details.
Reference genome sizes may need verification against the user's exact assembly and read filtering strategy.

use_cases.title: ChIP-seq Quality Review
use_cases.description: Build a QC workflow with correlation, PCA, coverage, fragment size, and fingerprint plots before downstream interpretation.

use_cases.title: Genome Browser Track Generation
use_cases.description: Convert aligned BAM files into normalized bigWig tracks for visual inspection in genome browsers.

use_cases.title: ATAC-seq Signal Visualization
use_cases.description: Create an ATAC-seq workflow with Tn5 correction, coverage generation, fragment checks, and peak-centered heatmaps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I have BAM files from a sequencing experiment. Ask me the minimum questions needed, then recommend the right deepTools workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Convert BAM to bigWig
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a deepTools bamCoverage command for my experiment. Explain the normalization choice, genome size, bin size, and read filtering options.

prompt_templates.title: Assess ChIP-seq Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a ChIP-seq QC plan using deepTools. Include correlation, PCA, coverage, fragment size, and enrichment checks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Complete Visualization Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a reproducible deepTools workflow from BAM files to normalized tracks, comparison tracks, matrices, heatmaps, and profile plots.

output_examples.input: User asks how to compare treatment and control ChIP-seq BAM files.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends bamCoverage for normalized tracks, bamCompare for log2 ratios, and plotFingerprint before interpretation.

output_examples.input: User asks which normalization method to use for RNA-seq coverage.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains CPM for fixed-width coverage bins and RPKM for gene-level summaries, with a warning not to extend RNA-seq reads.

output_examples.input: User asks for a heatmap around transcription start sites.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines computeMatrix reference-point followed by plotHeatmap, including upstream distance, downstream distance, and clustering options.

best_practices: Validate BAM, bigWig, and BED files before running analysis workflows.
Use consistent normalization and genome assembly values across samples being compared.
Review generated shell scripts and quote file paths before running them.
anti_patterns: Do not use RNA-seq read extension across splice junctions.
Do not compare samples with inconsistent genome builds or normalization methods.
Do not run generated workflows with untrusted filenames or directory names.

faq.question: What is deepTools used for?
faq.answer: deepTools processes sequencing alignments, creates coverage tracks, checks quality, compares samples, and generates genome signal visualizations.

faq.question: Which experiment types does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers ChIP-seq, RNA-seq coverage, ATAC-seq, MNase-seq, and related NGS signal analysis workflows.

faq.question: Can it generate runnable workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow generator, but generated shell scripts should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Does it validate input files?
faq.answer: Yes. The validation helper checks file existence, BAM index files, basic bigWig size, and BED formatting.

faq.question: How should I choose normalization?
faq.answer: Use the included normalization guidance. Common choices include RPGC for genome-normalized coverage and CPM for fixed-bin comparisons.

faq.question: Is this safe for sensitive sequencing data?
faq.answer: The skill does not exfiltrate data, but commands process local files. Review scripts and avoid untrusted input paths.
